This is Charles, and I'm one of the surgeons at Southpaws.
Today, we've got an interesting case, which is a large chondrosarcoma on the chest wall of a dog.
It was an incidental finding several weeks ago, and the primary care vet did a biopsy, found that it was a low-grade chondrosarcoma.
Chondrosarcomas are one of the three common tumors we see on the chest wall of dogs.
The other two being soft tissue sarcoma and osteosarcoma.
And then occasionally we will see hemangiosarcoma as well.
And tumors that occur on the chest wall are going to be similar in behavior to if they were to occur in other places.
So osteosarcomas have a median survival time of about a year with surgery and chemotherapy.
soft tissue sarcomas have a median survival time of about four years with surgery alone.
And chondrosarcomas, in most cases, are going to behave very similarly to a soft tissue sarcoma.
So we're going to get that same survival time, which is approximately four years with surgery alone.
survival with chondrosarcomas is grade-dependent, with high-grade tumors having a poorer survival than low- and intermediate-grade tumors.

So Jackson Pratt is my favorite kind of chest tube.
And the reason is that number one, it's very soft and so it's not going to be painful as it goes around the ribs.
It's also very unlikely to get kinked off, unlike an argyle chest catheter, which can get kinked off because it's more brittle, more stiff.
And then the other big advantage is that there's so many fenestrations in a Jackson-Pratt that it's very unlikely to get occluded with lung, whereas an argyle catheter only has a few fenestrations.
